attributes.stem:
A 51-year-old man presents to his general practice clinic with a two day history of a painful, itchy, red rash. He reports that he has been having difficulty eating over the past four months due to multiple slow-healing mouth ulcers. There is no history of genital lesions, vision changes, or stool changes. The patient denies any recent fever, lethargy or myalgia. He has no known significant medical conditions or medications.

On examination the rash is evident with erythematous patches and flaccid blisters and erosions (Image 1, 2). Several erosions can be seen on the buccal mucosa bilaterally, with some involvement to the tongue and soft palate.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
There is an erythematous, painful rash with crusted, eroded lesions. Combined with the history of mucosal involvement, this is a classic presentation of Pemphigus vulgaris.

Pemphigus vulgaris is the most common type of pemphigus that usually affects individuals from 50-60, characterized by intraepidermal blisters that involve both the skin and mucous membranes. There is oral mucosa involvement in 50-70% of cases, and the classic presentation often begins with these oral lesions that are present for weeks to months before any progression to skin involvement, if at all. The skin rash begins with erythematous macules that progress to painful blisters which rupture easily and leave erosions and crusts. It can be life-threatening without treatment and should be investigated early.

Bullous pemphigoid typically occurs in more elderly patients (>70), and there are large, tense, fluid-filled blisters that form. Although this could be the former stages of disease, where there is an itchy, red rash, there is not typically mucosal involvement, which is not consistent with this patient's intra-oral examination.

Erythema multiforme major may be considered, given the a widespread erythematous rash and involvement of both skin and mucous membranes. However the absence of the classic 'target-like lesions' and significant history (e.g. new medications, recent infections, symptoms of fever/lethargy/myalgia) make this less likely.

Discoid lupus erythematosus can have oral mucosa involvement and cause lesions that form crusts. However, it typically affects women from 20-40 and classically causes painless, dry, discoid lesions that evolve to form red plaques with scale, which this image does not show. Additionally, it would usually affect the face and sun exposed areas rather than the back or limbs.

Dermatitis herpetiformis is a condition associated with coeliac disease, which this patient is not known to have. It causes itchy, vesicular lesions which typically affect the extensor surfaces (e.g. knees/elbows/buttocks) rather than the back, and does not affect mucosa.
learningPoints: Pemphigus vulgaris is a potentially life-threatening condition that should be suspected and investigated if there is skin and mucosal involvement
Pemphigus vulgaris classically presents with blisters and erosions that initially affect the oral mucosa
references: Oakley A, Shakeel M. Pemphigus vulgaris [Internet]. 2022 [cited 2023 Aug 16]. Available from: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/pemphigus-vulgaris
Murrell D, Welsh B. ACD A-Z of Skin - Pemphigus vulgaris [Internet]. 2023 [cited 2023 Aug 16]. Available from: https://www.dermcoll.edu.au/atoz/pemphigus-vulgaris/
Welsh B. Blistering skin conditions. Australian Family Physicians. 2009 Jul;38(7):484–90.
